evidence: buildExploreResponseBody and buildBaseExploreResponse centralize shared response assembly for both single-seed and multi-seed paths, so the handler seam has no duplication
All model-to-JSON translation (formatDiscoveredNoun, formatCoreCollocate, formatAdjectiveProfile, formatEdge) is concentrated inside ExplorationHandlers, not leaked into model or query packages
ExploreOptions record bundles tuning parameters cleanly, removing a 5-argument list from the exploreByPattern call site
CollocateQueryHelper.buildAndRankCollocates is a shared ranking seam reused verbatim by findCollocations, executeDependencyPattern, and executeSurfacePattern, keeping logDice scoring in one place

exploreMultiSeed in SemanticFieldExplorer maps multi-seed results into ExplorationResult with seed set to String.join(',', seeds); ExplorationResult.getSeed() then returns 'theory,model,hypothesis', a string that does not represent a single seed — the handler must compensate by adding separate 'seeds' and 'seed_count' keys outside the standard buildExploreResponseBody path

In BlackLabQueryExecutor.findCollocations, when BlackLabSnippetParser.extractLemmaFromMatch returns null, the fallback uses ad-hoc lastIndexOf(' ') string splitting (trimmed.substring(lastSpace + 1)) rather than any shared utility; executeDependencyPattern has no equivalent fallback, so extraction strategy is inconsistent across methods at the executor/parser boundary

ExplorationResult.getEdges() and ComparisonResult.getEdges() build visualization edges directly in model classes, embedding edge-type strings ('seed_adj', 'discovered_adj', 'modifier') that are presentation concerns; the handler then calls result.getEdges() and re-maps through formatEdge(), meaning edge construction logic is split between model and formatter

SemanticFieldExplorer.fetchExamples builds a hardcoded BCQL pattern '[lemma="%s" & xpos="JJ.*"] []{0,3} [lemma="%s" & xpos="NN.*"]' inline, bypassing RelationConfig.getFullPattern() entirely; every other exploration method delegates to RelationConfig, so this method silently breaks the config→explorer contract
BlackLabSnippetParser has ~35 targeted unit tests covering every parsing method including null/empty edge cases — one of the most thoroughly tested classes.

SemanticFieldExplorer and AdjectiveCollocateComparator are each tested with a consistent StubExecutor pattern: stubs implement QueryExecutor, returning canned collocate lists, so tests exercise real intersection/sorting logic without a live index.
LogDiceCalculator tests cover formula correctness, symmetry, monotonicity, extreme-ratio edge cases, and realistic corpus frequency scenarios (250M-token simulations).

API handler validation tests (HandlersTest) verify all missing-parameter 400 paths and one happy-path 200 shape for both SketchHandlers and ExplorationHandlers using a hand-crafted MockExchange — no snapshot usage.

CollocateQueryHelper (src/main/java/.../query/CollocateQueryHelper.java) is a 200+ line class containing the shared collocate search pipeline (executeCollocateSearch, buildAndRankCollocates, collectHits, scoreHits, executeBcqlQuery) — it has zero direct tests and zero indirect tests because BlackLabQueryExecutorTest is class-level @Disabled.

BlackLabQueryExecutorTest bears a class-level @Disabled('Requires live BlackLab index') annotation, meaning its tests never execute in any CI run. Tests are written and documented, but perpetually disabled rather than conditionally skipped (compare: DependencySketchTest uses Assumptions.assumeTrue which is a superior pattern).
RelationUtils (alias resolution map) has no tests, though it is trivial and pure.
BlackLabConllUIndexer (indexing pipeline) and RadialPlot (SVG generation) have zero test coverage.
No snapshot tests exist in the suite — all assertions are explicit equality/contains checks.
No timing- or ordering-dependent tests found; StubExecutor is deterministic and stateless.
QueryExecutor interface: all 5 collocate-returning methods use consistent (lemma, pattern, minLogDice, maxResults) parameter ordering — clean.

SemanticFieldExplorer has three public methods with incompatible parameter conventions: exploreByPattern uses ExploreOptions value object, but exploreMultiSeed uses 5 flat primitives (seeds, config, minLogDice, topCollocates, minShared), and compareCollocateProfiles uses (nouns, minLogDice, maxPerNoun) with different param names for the same concepts.

Naming inconsistency in SemanticFieldExplorer public API: 'seeds' (exploreByPattern, exploreMultiSeed) vs 'nouns' (compareCollocateProfiles), 'topCollocates' (exploreMultiSeed, ExploreOptions) vs 'maxPerNoun' (compareCollocateProfiles) for logically equivalent limits.

Model layer uses mixed accessor styles: record types (AdjectiveProfile, CoreCollocate, ExploreOptions) use no-prefix accessor x(), while ComparisonResult (class) uses getX() JavaBeans style. ComparisonResult Javadoc acknowledges this but API consumers still face the disparity.

QueryResults.CollocateResult extends ConcordanceResult sealed interface, yet adds collocate-specific fields (collocateLemma, frequency, logDice) not present in the other ConcordanceResult subtypes — it is a collocation result masquerading as a concordance result.
HttpApiUtils static helpers are coherent: all take (HttpExchange, ...) with consistent ordering and uniform Javadoc error contracts.
All query-path public methods declare throws IOException consistently; error handling at the HTTP boundary is uniform via sendError/sendJsonResponse.

strengths: Translation at the handler boundary is consolidated: all format* helpers are package-private statics in ExplorationHandlers, so domain objects never carry JSON-map building logic
buildExploreResponseBody extracts the shared response structure shared by single-seed and multi-seed handlers, avoiding divergent copy-paste at the HTTP seam
ExploreOptions record eliminates a long parameter list at the SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern seam, making the handoff explicit and minimal
CollocateQueryHelper.buildAndRankCollocates is a clean shared scoring seam reused by all three executor query variants without duplication
HttpApiUtils.requireParam provides a consistent null-sentinel gateway pattern for parameter extraction, with a predictable single-check protocol across all handlers
mid_level_elegance.issue_character: Issues are concentrated at two seams — the multi-seed data mapping into a single-seed-shaped result type, and a pair of bypasses (ad-hoc extraction fallback, hardcoded BCQL in fetchExamples) that create invisible inconsistencies at the executor/config boundaries — rather than being a systemic breakdown across the whole module.
mid_level_elegance.score_rationale: The codebase sits above the 60-point drag threshold: boundary translation is generally disciplined, helpers are well-factored, and the three-layer handoff (API → exploration → query) is legible. However, the ExplorationResult reuse for multi-seed data forces the handler to patch around the model's semantic mismatch, and two config/extraction bypasses create invisible seam inconsistencies that a developer will stumble on. These are not isolated micro-issues — each affects how collaborators communicate — but they are bounded to specific seams rather than pervasive, which keeps the score from falling further. Solid-but-uneven lands around 67.

strengths: Consistent StubExecutor/MockExchange pattern isolates business logic from infrastructure: SemanticFieldExplorer, AdjectiveCollocateComparator, and API handlers all test real logic via well-designed test doubles.
BlackLabSnippetParser unit tests are thorough and exhaustive, covering all public methods with null/empty/boundary cases.
LogDiceCalculator tests are unusually strong, including formula properties (symmetry, monotonicity), real corpus simulations, and overflow/underflow guards.
No snapshot test overuse: every assertion is an explicit check, making tests self-documenting and refactor-safe.
DependencySketchTest correctly uses Assumptions.assumeTrue so tests self-skip when no index is present, rather than being permanently disabled.
test_strategy.issue_character: The test gaps are structural rather than scattered: the deepest layer of the query execution stack (CollocateQueryHelper, BlackLabQueryExecutor) is systematically untested because it was extracted into a class that inherits the live-index dependency, and the class-level @Disabled on BlackLabQueryExecutorTest means no coverage ever executes in CI — this is a single root cause manifesting as a complete blind spot on the most complex code path.
test_strategy.score_rationale: The test suite is genuinely good for the pure and domain-logic code (~70% of the codebase by count), with well-designed stubs and no fragility patterns. However, CollocateQueryHelper — the shared infrastructure class encapsulating collocate search, frequency ranking, and hit scoring — has zero test coverage, and the entire BlackLabQueryExecutor layer is class-level @Disabled. This leaves the most algorithmically complex code path (multi-phase hit collection → frequency map → logDice ranking) completely unverified. Compared to the global anchors: the suite is well above 40 (which would indicate widespread absence of tests) but falls short of 65 (significant drag) because the gap is concentrated at one critical integration seam rather than spread across everything. A score of 54 reflects solid unit test discipline undercut by a complete blind spot on the live-query infrastructure path.

strengths: QueryExecutor interface is a well-shaped, consistently documented contract: lemma-first parameter ordering, uniform IOException declaration, and clear Javadoc distinguishing four conceptually similar methods.
HttpApiUtils is a clean static-utility surface: all methods take HttpExchange as first arg, send consistent error shapes, and are fully documented.
ExploreOptions value object was introduced to consolidate repeated flat parameters into exploreByPattern — the intent toward coherence is visible.
api_surface_coherence.issue_character: The inconsistencies are concentrated in the SemanticFieldExplorer public API, where ExploreOptions bundling was applied to only one of three public methods, leaving the others with flat primitives and divergent names for the same concepts — a partially completed refactor rather than systemic disorder.
api_surface_coherence.score_rationale: The QueryExecutor interface and HTTP utility layer are solid and coherent, which is a meaningful strength at the integration boundary. However, SemanticFieldExplorer's public API has a visible split: exploreByPattern was refactored to use ExploreOptions but exploreMultiSeed and compareCollocateProfiles were not, leaving three public methods with incompatible shapes and inconsistent param names ('seeds' vs 'nouns', 'topCollocates' vs 'maxPerNoun'). Additionally, CollocateResult conflating concordance and collocate concerns, and the mixed record/class accessor styles in the model layer, add real friction for consumers. This places the codebase below 'solid but uneven' (80) — there are identifiable defects at an important API boundary — but above 'significant drag' (60) because the query-layer interface itself is clean and the HTTP surface is coherent.

issues.summary: exploreMultiSeed maps multi-seed data into single-seed ExplorationResult, forcing the handler to compensate with extra keys outside the shared response path
issues.suggestion: Introduce a dedicated MultiSeedExplorationResult (or add a seeds: List<String> field to ExplorationResult alongside seed) so the type correctly carries multi-seed data, and buildExploreResponseBody can handle both cases uniformly without out-of-band handler patching.
evidence: exploreMultiSeed returns new ExplorationResult(String.join(',', seeds), ...) — getSeed() returns 'theory,model,hypothesis', not a single seed

handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti calls buildExploreResponseBody(response, result) but then adds response.put('seeds', ...) and response.put('seed_count', ...) outside of it, because the shared helper only writes the single-seed key 'seed' on the single-seed path
The seeds become discoveredNouns inside ExplorationResult, even though they are known inputs, not discovered outputs — the type's meaning is semantically stretched

issues.summary: findCollocations uses an ad-hoc lastIndexOf(' ') fallback for lemma extraction not shared with executeDependencyPattern, scattering identity-parsing logic across two call sites
issues.suggestion: Move the fallback strategy into BlackLabSnippetParser (e.g., extractLemmaFromMatchWithFallback), so all executor methods share one extraction seam and the fallback logic is visible and testable in one place.
evidence: In findCollocations: if extractLemmaFromMatch returns null, the code does trimmed.lastIndexOf(' ') and takes the suffix — raw string splitting with no encapsulation
executeDependencyPattern calls extractCollocateLemma(identity) with no fallback — the two methods handle a null result differently with no shared strategy
BlackLabSnippetParser already has extractCollocateLemma and extractLemmaAt for positional extraction, so the fallback is bypassing the canonical extraction seam

issues.summary: ExplorationResult.getEdges() and ComparisonResult.getEdges() embed visualization edge construction — including presentation-level edge type strings — inside model classes
issues.suggestion: Remove getEdges() from both model classes and move edge construction into ExplorationHandlers (or a dedicated EdgeBuilder), so model classes carry only data and the handler owns the full model-to-response translation in one place.
evidence: ExplorationResult.getEdges() creates Edge objects with type 'seed_adj' and 'discovered_adj' — these string labels are visualization/presentation conventions, not domain invariants
ComparisonResult.getEdges() similarly creates Edge('modifier') labels inside the model
The handler then iterates result.getEdges() and re-maps each Edge through formatEdge(), so the model builds edges and the handler formats them, splitting the boundary logic across two layers

issues.summary: SemanticFieldExplorer.fetchExamples hardcodes a BCQL pattern instead of delegating to RelationConfig, silently breaking the grammar-config contract
issues.suggestion: Accept a RelationConfig parameter in fetchExamples (or use a default config from grammarConfig) and derive the example query from it, consistent with all other exploration methods. Alternatively, expose the hardcoded pattern as a named constant with a comment explaining why it is relation-agnostic.
evidence: fetchExamples builds: "[lemma=\"%s\" & xpos=\"JJ.*\"] []{0,3} [lemma=\"%s\" & xpos=\"NN.*\"]" — hardcoded xpos tags and window size with no config injection
Every other exploration method uses relationConfig.getFullPattern(seed) or relationConfig.collocateReversePattern() to look up patterns from grammar config
The API handler (handleSemanticFieldExamples) does not take a relation parameter, so there is no way to vary the pattern even if a caller wanted to

issues.summary: CollocateQueryHelper has zero test coverage despite containing the shared collocate-ranking pipeline
issues.suggestion: Extract the pure scoring logic from buildAndRankCollocates into a static method (or separate scorer class) that takes a frequency map and returns ranked results — this breaks the index dependency and allows direct unit testing with hand-crafted frequency maps. Add tests for the boundary cases: empty frequency map, minLogDice filtering, maxResults truncation, and ties.
evidence: CollocateQueryHelper contains buildAndRankCollocates (frequency-map → logDice scoring), collectHits (multi-phase hit iteration), scoreHits (per-hit logDice computation), and executeBcqlQuery (full two-phase pipeline) — all non-trivial logic.
No test file for CollocateQueryHelper exists anywhere under src/test.
buildAndRankCollocates and collectHits could be tested with a mock BlackLabIndex or by extracting the frequency-map → result-list transformation into a pure function that takes no index dependency.
BlackLabQueryExecutorTest is class-level @Disabled, so CollocateQueryHelper is never exercised indirectly either.

issues.summary: BlackLabQueryExecutorTest uses class-level @Disabled, permanently suppressing all executor tests in CI
issues.suggestion: Replace the class-level @Disabled on BlackLabQueryExecutorTest with Assumptions.assumeTrue(new File(INDEX_PATH).exists(), 'Skipping: index not found at ' + INDEX_PATH) inside a @BeforeAll or at the start of each test, matching the pattern already used in DependencySketchTest. This makes the tests automatically activate when the index is present without any annotation removal.
evidence: BlackLabQueryExecutorTest has @Disabled at class level: '@Disabled("Requires live BlackLab index — set CONCEPT_SKETCH_TEST_INDEX env var to enable")'. Every method is also individually @Disabled.
DependencySketchTest uses Assumptions.assumeTrue(indexDir.exists(), ...) — a strictly better pattern: tests run when the env is available and skip gracefully otherwise.
BlackLabBcqlConcordanceTest uses @EnabledIf('indexExists') — also correct.
With class-level @Disabled, the intent to 'document expected behavior' is achieved, but the tests provide zero safety net even in environments where CONCEPT_SKETCH_TEST_INDEX is set.

issues.summary: RelationUtils alias resolution has no test despite being a pure function used by both handler classes
issues.suggestion: Add a small parametrized test covering all 8 known aliases, the pass-through case for an unknown value, and the null-input case — this is a trivial addition with high value given the method is on a user-input path.
evidence: RelationUtils.resolveRelationAlias is a pure static method mapping 8 known aliases (e.g. 'adj_predicate' → 'noun_adj_predicates') with a pass-through for unknown values.
No test exists for this method; grep for 'RelationUtils' in src/test returns no matches.
Both ExplorationHandlers and SketchHandlers call resolveRelationAlias on user-supplied input; a mistaken alias entry would silently return wrong relation configs.

issues.summary: SemanticFieldExplorer public API splits between ExploreOptions object and flat primitives across sibling methods
issues.suggestion: Extend or adapt ExploreOptions (or a parallel MultiSeedOptions record) to cover multi-seed and comparison calls, so all three public methods accept a shared options type at consistent argument position. At minimum, make exploreMultiSeed accept ExploreOptions to match exploreByPattern.
evidence: exploreByPattern(String seed, RelationConfig config, ExploreOptions opts) uses the ExploreOptions value object

exploreMultiSeed(Set<String> seeds, RelationConfig config, double minLogDice, int topCollocates, int minShared) uses 5 flat primitives — the same logical parameters as ExploreOptions minus 'nounsPerSeed'
compareCollocateProfiles(Set<String> nouns, double minLogDice, int maxPerNoun) uses a different flat-param shape again

issues.summary: The same concept is named 'seeds' in two public methods and 'nouns' in a third, with parallel rename of the count limit
issues.suggestion: Rename compareCollocateProfiles parameter 'nouns' to 'seeds' and 'maxPerNoun' to 'topCollocates' to match the vocabulary used elsewhere in the same class.
evidence: exploreByPattern first param is 'seed' (single), exploreMultiSeed first param is 'seeds' — consistent
compareCollocateProfiles first param is 'nouns' for the same Set<String> concept
exploreMultiSeed uses 'topCollocates'; compareCollocateProfiles uses 'maxPerNoun' for the equivalent result-count ceiling

issues.summary: CollocateResult extends ConcordanceResult sealed interface despite adding fields unrelated to concordance
issues.suggestion: Remove CollocateResult from the ConcordanceResult sealed hierarchy and make it a standalone record. If callers need to treat both as concordance results, introduce a common supertype or use an explicit conversion method.
evidence: QueryResults.CollocateResult implements ConcordanceResult (getSentence, startOffset, endOffset, docId) but adds collocateLemma, frequency, and logDice — collocate-ranking fields absent from SnippetResult and KwicResult

executeBcqlQuery returns List<CollocateResult> as its own return type distinct from executeQuery's List<ConcordanceResult>, so CollocateResult does not need to be a ConcordanceResult subtype to be usable
